content:
Some clarifying questions / comments from me:

[Core Logic]

- What's the difference between a position and an index? Do they both refer to the same value (from 1 to N)?

- Assuming position = index, all positions 1 < P ≤ N is guaranteed to have a smaller consecutive position, which is just P - 1. Am I missing something here? Why isn't the result for all possible N just 1 (i.e. only the first position survives every time)?

[Problem Statement]

- If the filtering operation is only applied once, you can remove the description of a "filtering operation", and simply tell the player to "eliminate any position whose..."

- Please format all instances of N as [[N]].

❌ Rejected the contribution.
Comment: * The statement is ambiguous about how the filtering operation should be applied. For example, one interpretation consistent with the wording is to process indices sequentially from the end:
  • Pair N and N - 1 and eliminate N
  • Pair N - 1 and N - 2 and eliminate N - 1
  • Pair N - 2 and N - 3 and eliminate N - 2
and so on. Under this interpretation, the answer is always 1.

* Since the result can be obtained by a direct calculation (based on the author's solution), it currently lacks any key programming elements such as lists, sets, dictionaries, string manipulation, loops, or conditions.

Quick logic puzzle! suits well for shortest/reverse mode. But I'd suggest specifying that the pairs are disjoint (1-2, 3-4...), as the current wording could be interpreted as a domino effect (Luckily, the example clears up the ambiguity).

Thanks for the suggestion. The problem was mainly designed for reverse mode, but it is also valid for other modes. I’ve included additional test cases to ensure the expected behavior is clear and to minimize ambiguity
